Croatia::Zadar::Kolovare Beach.description: Kolovare is Zadar’s main public city beach, a few minutes east of the old town along a calm stretch of the Adriatic popular with locals for swimming and evening walks.

Croatia::Rijeka::Molo Longo.description: Molo Longo is Rijeka’s long breakwater pier, offering open-sea views back toward the city, the harbor, and the Kvarner Gulf — a favorite spot for sunset strolls.

Croatia::Pula::Zerostrasse.description: Zerostrasse is a network of preserved World War I tunnels beneath central Pula, built as air-raid shelters and now open as an atmospheric underground route through the city’s history.

Croatia::Rovinj::Lone Bay.description: Lone Bay is a sheltered pebble cove on the Rovinj peninsula, framed by pines and clear Adriatic water — a quieter alternative to the busy old-town beaches.

Croatia::Rovinj::Grisia Street.description: Grisia is Rovinj’s steep, cobbled lane of galleries and artist studios, famous for its open-air painting exhibition each summer and views toward the church on the hill.

Croatia::Osijek::Sakuntala Park.description: Šakuntala Park is a green pocket in Osijek’s Donji grad, named after the Indian literary figure and laid out as a quiet retreat among the city’s Habsburg-era streets.

Czech Republic::Kutná Hora::Sankturin Ho.description: The Sankturin House is a Baroque burgher palace on Kutná Hora’s Palackého náměstí, notable for its stucco façade and role as a fine example of the town’s silver-era merchant architecture.

Czech Republic::Karlovy Vary::Smetana Ga.description: Smetana Gardens is a terraced park above Karlovy Vary’s colonnades, planted with alleys and lookout points over the spa valley and the Teplá River.

Czech Republic::Hradec Králové::Park 360.description: Park 360 is a modern riverside event space on the Elbe in Hradec Králové, used for festivals and concerts with wide views of the city skyline and waterfront.

Czech Republic::Hradec Králové::Jirásek.description: Jirásek Gardens is a formal city park in central Hradec Králové, named after the Czech writer Alois Jirásek and popular for shaded walks near the historic core.

Czech Republic::Hradec Králové::Klicpero.description: Klicperovo Theatre is the main drama stage in Hradec Králové, housed in a neo-Renaissance building and anchoring the city’s cultural calendar since the late 19th century.

Czech Republic::Olomouc::Bezručovy sady.description: Bezručovy sady is a riverside park along the Morava in Olomouc, lined with paths and views toward the old town — a local favorite for jogging and weekend picnics.

Czech Republic::Liberec::iQLANDIA.description: iQLANDIA is an interactive science center in Liberec with hands-on exhibits, a planetarium, and panoramic views from its modern building near the city center.

Bosnia and Herzegovina::Tuzla::Tuzla Sal.description: Tuzla’s saltworks recall the city’s ancient name — ‘place of salt’ — where brine has been evaporated for centuries and still shapes local identity and spa culture.

Bosnia and Herzegovina::Jajce::Pliva Lak.description: The Pliva Lakes near Jajce are two emerald reservoirs linked by channels and mills, ideal for kayaking, walking, and the wooden watermills that define this landscape.

Bosnia and Herzegovina::Jajce::Jajce Old.description: Jajce’s old town crowns a plateau above the Pliva waterfall, with medieval walls, royal history as the last Bosnian capital, and stone streets overlooking the gorge.

Bosnia and Herzegovina::Neum::Klek Mount.description: Klek is a limestone ridge above the Neum corridor, rising sharply from the coast and visible from much of Bosnia’s short Adriatic strip.

Bosnia and Herzegovina::Neum::Klek Fortr.description: The Klek fortress ruins sit on a strategic hill above Neum, guarding the narrow coastal passage that connects southern Dalmatia with the rest of Bosnia and Herzegovina.

Bosnia and Herzegovina::Neum::Neum Bay.description: Neum Bay opens onto Bosnia’s only sea access, a deep inlet sheltered by hills and lined with hotels and beaches that make the town the country’s Adriatic resort.

Bosnia and Herzegovina::Neum::Neum Beach.description: Neum’s beaches are pebble-and-shingle coves along the country’s 20-kilometre coastline, with calm, warm water that draws families from across the region each summer.

Bosnia and Herzegovina::Travnik::Ivo And.description: The Ivo Andrić Museum occupies the Nobel laureate’s birthplace in Travnik, preserving the rooms and objects tied to the writer’s childhood in this Ottoman-era town.

Bosnia and Herzegovina::Travnik::Vezir's.description: The Vezir’s Mosque in Travnik dates to the town’s peak as seat of Bosnian viziers, with a courtyard and minaret that still anchor the historic Muslim quarter.

Bosnia and Herzegovina::Travnik::Turbe o.description: The turbe in Travnik is an Ottoman mausoleum beside the old citadel, holding the remains of local dignitaries and reflecting the town’s centuries as a political center.

Bosnia and Herzegovina::Travnik::Travnik.description: Travnik’s clock tower rises above the čaršija, one of the few remaining Ottoman timekeepers in Bosnia and a landmark visible from across the valley.
Travnik Castle (Stari grad) is a medieval fortress on the hilltop that controlled the Lašva valley, later rebuilt under Ottoman rule and now offering wide views over the town.
Travnik’s old town winds through čaršija lanes of mosques, fountains, and colorful houses — the best-preserved Ottoman urban core in central Bosnia.

Bosnia and Herzegovina::Travnik::Sulejma.description: The Šarena (Painted) Mosque in Travnik is famous for its vividly decorated interior and exterior frescoes, one of the most distinctive Ottoman monuments in Bosnia.

Bosnia and Herzegovina::Mostar::Old Brid.description: The Old Bridge Museum beside Stari Most explains how the 16th-century Ottoman bridge was built, destroyed in 1993, and painstakingly rebuilt using original techniques.

Bosnia and Herzegovina::Mostar::Hamam Mu.description: Mostar’s hamam museum occupies a restored Turkish bath in the old town, showing how public bathing shaped daily life on both banks of the Neretva.

Bosnia and Herzegovina::Mostar::Kujundži.description: Kujundžiluk is Mostar’s copper-smith street in the old bazaar, where workshops still hammer trays, coffee sets, and souvenirs in a tradition dating to Ottoman times.

Bosnia and Herzegovina::Mostar::Bišćević.description: The Bišćević House is a preserved Ottoman residence in Mostar with original wooden screens (musandera) and a courtyard that opens toward the Neretva gorge.

Bosnia and Herzegovina::Banja Luka::Gosp.description: Gospodska Street is Banja Luka’s main pedestrian promenade, lined with cafés, shops, and Habsburg-era façades that define the city’s everyday social life.

Bosnia and Herzegovina::Banja Luka::Peta.description: Petar Kočić Park is a central green space in Banja Luka named after the Serb writer, popular for shaded benches and events beside the city’s main cultural institutions.

Bosnia and Herzegovina::Sarajevo::Yellow.description: The Yellow Fortress (Žuta tabija) is an Ottoman bastion on Sarajevo’s eastern hillside, best known today as the city’s favorite sunset viewpoint over the valley.

France::Nice::Monastère de Cimiez.description: The Monastery of Cimiez sits on a hill above Nice with a church, cloister, and small museum, surrounded by gardens and Roman ruins in one of the city’s quietest historic quarters.

France::Lille::Zoo de Lille.description: Lille Zoo is a compact city zoo in the Citadel park, home to around 70 species and a popular family stop within walking distance of the old town.
